ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a Thai combo set meal. FOODON_00002473 (meal food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00FL (composite meal) is the closest parent. No specific Thai combo set meal code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for a Thai combo set meal. SNOMED CT does not enumerate restaurant meal combinations at this specificity.

main_ingredients: protein (chicken, pork, tofu, or seafood depending on selection)
mango cubes or coconut sago dessert

serving.natural_language_portion: One full Thai combo set meal comprising a rice or noodle main dish (approximately 350 g), a sweetened drink (approximately 200 ml), and a dessert of mango cubes or coconut sago (approximately 100 g), totalling approximately 650 g.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from: USDA FoodData Central — Pad Thai (SR Legacy 35188), cooked jasmine rice (SR Legacy 20444), mango (SR Legacy 09176), coconut milk (SR Legacy 12117), tapioca pearls (SR Legacy 20066), and sweetened Thai iced drink analogue; weighted by estimated component proportions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite Thai combo set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component averaging: main dish ~54% (Pad Thai or jasmine rice with stir-fry), drink ~31% (sweetened Thai drink), dessert ~15% (mango cubes or coconut sago). Per-100g values are derived from this blended estimate. Confidence is medium. Values should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis or HPB Singapore composite meal data if available.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of rice-based and noodle-based Thai dishes — analogous estimate based on: Jasmine rice GI 68 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Am J Clin Nutr); Pad Thai noodles GI ~53 (rice noodles, University of Sydney GI Database); composite weighted estimate for mixed meal
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific Thai combo set meal. GI is estimated as a weighted composite: jasmine rice GI 68 (main dish component, ~54% weight), rice noodles GI 53 (alternative main, averaged), sweetened drink GI ~65 (sucrose-based beverage analogue), mango GI 51 and coconut sago GI ~46 (dessert component, ~15% weight). Weighted composite GI ≈ 62, classified as medium. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 120.25) / 100 ≈ 74.5 g GL per 650 g serving. Carbohydrate per serving = 18.5 × 6.5 = 120.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, soy sauce, and seasoning in Thai main dishes
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this combo meal to occasional consumption, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 310 mg per 100 g (approximately 2015 mg per full serving) substantially approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce preparation and avoiding added condiments is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving driven by refined rice or rice noodles, sweetened drink, and sugary dessert components
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this combo meal due to its high glycemic load (estimated GL ≈ 74.5 per serving) and the combined sugar contribution from the sweetened drink and mango or coconut sago dessert; substituting the drink with unsweetened water and choosing a smaller portion of the main dish can meaningfully reduce postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from coconut milk in sago dessert and cooking oils, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be aware that this combo meal contributes moderate saturated fat (estimated 1.4 g per 100 g, approximately 9.1 g per serving) primarily from coconut milk and cooking oil, alongside high sodium; selecting the mango dessert over coconut sago and requesting less oil in the main dish preparation can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Rice or Noodle Combo Meal with Drink and Dessert is a complete Thai restaurant set meal offered at Saap Saap Thai in Singapore. The meal comprises three components: (1) a main dish — the diner's choice of a Thai rice dish (such as basil fried rice or pineapple fried rice) or a Thai noodle dish (such as Pad Thai or boat noodles), typically stir-fried or braised with Thai seasonings including fish sauce, oyster sauce, and aromatic herbs; (2) a refreshing sweetened drink, commonly a Thai iced tea, lemongrass drink, or similar Thai beverage; and (3) a dessert — either fresh mango cubes or violet coconut sago, a chilled Thai dessert made from tapioca pearls cooked in sweetened coconut milk with butterfly pea flower colouring. The overall flavour profile is savoury and umami-rich in the main, sweet and aromatic in the drink, and creamy or fruity in the dessert. Nutritionally, this is a high-carbohydrate, moderate-protein, moderate-sodium meal with a medium glycemic index of approximately 62 and a high glycemic load per serving of approximately 74.5 g. The sodium content is elevated due to Thai seasoning sauces. Saturated fat is moderate, contributed by coconut milk in the sago dessert and cooking oil in the main dish. The meal is suitable for healthy adults as an occasional indulgence but requires portion and component management for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is not suitable for those with peanut, shellfish, soy, or fish allergies without modification.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific Thai combo set meal as served at Saap Saap Thai Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component averaging of USDA FoodData Central entries for the constituent food types (Pad Thai, jasmine rice, mango, coconut milk, tapioca, sweetened drink). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for jasmine rice and rice noodles. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are individually well-supported by USDA SR Legacy data and the estimation methodology is transparent and documented.
notes: 1. This record represents a composite Thai combo set meal (GD001528) as listed on field survey data by Saap Saap Thai at Funan. The meal is variable by design — the diner selects a main dish from a Thai rice or noodle menu, a drink, and a dessert. Nutrient values therefore represent a central estimate across likely selections. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the USDA component entries used for this composite estimate and varies substantially depending on protein selection. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for the primary components of this meal. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported consistently across the USDA component entries used. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible in Thai restaurant cooking using vegetable oils — confirmed near-zero per USDA SR Legacy component data. 6. GI is an analogous estimate (weighted composite ≈ 62, medium) based on jasmine rice GI 68 and rice noodle GI 53 from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. 7. Sodium estimate of ~310 mg per 100 g is based on Pad Thai and Thai stir-fry USDA data and is consistent with known high-sodium profiles of Thai restaurant dishes. 8. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Grains and staples' (input) to 'Mixed' as this is a composite set meal including a main, drink, and dessert. 9. Thai mandatory local-language name is present per G6 multilingual rule.
